coredata.dc:title: Remarkable synergetic effect by in-situ covalent hybridization of carbon dots with graphene oxide and carboxylated acrylonitrile butadiene rubber
coredata.dc:creator: Sreenath, P.R.
Mandal, Saptarshi
Singh, Seema
Das, Prolay
Bhowmick, Anil K.
Dinesh Kumar, K.
coredata.dc:description: This work investigates the remarkable synergetic effect produced by in-situ covalent hybridization of carbon dots (CDs) with graphene oxide (GO) and carboxylated acrylonitrile butadiene latex (XNBR). The carboxylic acid groups of GO and XNBR latex have been covalently conjugated to surface amine groups of CDs following carbodiimide chemistry. The covalent conjugation in XNBR-CDs-GO hybrid nanocomposite has been confirmed by Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR) and X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS). Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) and atomic force microscopy (AFM) images show the presence of covalently bonded CDs on the GO surface and also in the XNBR latex. The optical properties of the XNBR-CDs-GO hybrid nanocomposites have been characterized by ultraviolet (UV) – visible and fluorescence spectroscopy. The in-situ covalent hybridization of CDs with GO and XNBR latex increases the interactions between the nanofillers and the XNBR latex and also effectively prevents the stacking of GO sheets in XNBR latex. Covalently hybridized XNBR-CDs-GO nanocomposites display higher physico-mechanical properties and thermal stability when compared to XNBR-CDs and XNBR-GO nanocomposites.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Graphene oxide
Hybrid nanofiller
XNBR
Latex
Synergy

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Preparation of in-situ assembled hybrid XNBR-CDs-GO nanocomposites
Preparation of XNBR-GO and XNBR-CDs nanocomposites
Formation of XNBR-CDs, XNBR-GO and XNBR-CDs-GO nanocomposites
Analysis of covalent conjugation in XNBR-CDs-GO nanocomposite by FTIR and XPS
Morphological analysis by transmission electron microscopy (TEM) and atomic force microscopy (AFM)

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic representation of the formation of hybrid XNBR-CDs-GO nanocomposite.
(a) FTIR spectra of XNBR, GO and CDs and (b) FTIR spectra of XNBR-GO, XNBR-CDs, and XNBR-CDs-GO.
High resolution XPS C1s spectra of (a) XNBR, (b) XNBR-CDs-GO and N1s spectra of (c) XNBR and (d) XNBR-CDs-GO.

(a) TEM micrograph of XNBR+2CDs+2GO, (b) AFM topographic image of XNBR+2CDs+2GO, (c) TEM micrograph of XNBR+2CDs+4GO. Inset: TEM micrograph of XNBR+2CDs+4GO and (d) AFM topographic image of XNBR+2CDs+4GO.

(a) UV–visible spectra of neat XNBR latex, XNBR+2CDs nanocomposite and XNBR-CDs-GO hybrid nanocomposites, (b) Fluorescence spectra of neat XNBR latex, XNBR+2CDs nanocomposite and XNBR-CDs-GO hybrid nanocomposites and (c) Time-resolved fluorescence decay of XNBR+2CDs nanocomposite and XNBR+2CDs+2GO hybrid nanocomposite.
Digital images of solid neat XNBR, XNBR+2CDs, XNBR+2GO and XNBR+2CDs+2GO films taken under UV irradiation.
Tensile stress versus strain curves of XNBR, XNBR+2CDs, XNBR+2GO and XNBR+2CDs with various ratios of GO.

(a) Temperature versus tan δ plots of XNBR, XNBR+2CDs and XNBR+2CDs with various ratios of GO. Inset: Temperature versus loss modulus (G′′) plots of XNBR, XNBR+2CDs and XNBR+2CDs with various ratios of GO and (b) Temperature versus storage modulus (G′) plots of XNBR, XNBR+2CDs and XNBR+2CDs with various ratios of GO.
(a) TGA curves of neat XNBR, XNBR+2CDs and XNBR+2CDs with various ratios of GO and (b) DTG curves of neat XNBR, XNBR+2CDs and XNBR+2CDs with various ratios of GO.

ce:legend.ce:simple-para: *phr = parts per hundred ml of rubber latex.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Mechanical properties of neat XNBR, XNBR with various ratios of CDs, XNBR with various ratios of GO and XNBR+2CDs with various ratios of GO.
Rheological properties of neat XNBR, XNBR+2CDs, XNBR+2GO and XNBR+2CDs with various ratios of GO.

row.entry:
Tg from tan δ peak
temperature (oC)
Tg from loss modulus peak temperature (oC)

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Thermal stability studies of neat XNBR, XNBR+2CDs, XNBR+2GO and XNBR+2CDs with various ratios of GO.

head.ce:title: Remarkable synergetic effect by in-situ covalent hybridization of carbon dots with graphene oxide and carboxylated acrylonitrile butadiene rubber

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Metallurgical and Materials Engineering, Indian Institute of Technology, Patna, 801106, India

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Metallurgical and Materials Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Metallurgical and Materials Engineering, Indian Institute of Technology, Patna 801106, India

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, Indian Institute of Technology, Patna, 801106, India
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Chemistry, Indian Institute of Technology, Patna 801106, India

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Rubber Technology Centre, Indian Institute of Technology, Kharagpur, 721302, India
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Rubber Technology Centre, Indian Institute of Technology, Kharagpur 721302, India

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: This work investigates the remarkable synergetic effect produced by in-situ covalent hybridization of carbon dots (CDs) with graphene oxide (GO) and carboxylated acrylonitrile butadiene latex (XNBR). The carboxylic acid groups of GO and XNBR latex have been covalently conjugated to surface amine groups of CDs following carbodiimide chemistry. The covalent conjugation in XNBR-CDs-GO hybrid nanocomposite has been confirmed by Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR) and X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS). Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) and atomic force microscopy (AFM) images show the presence of covalently bonded CDs on the GO surface and also in the XNBR latex. The optical properties of the XNBR-CDs-GO hybrid nanocomposites have been characterized by ultraviolet (UV) – visible and fluorescence spectroscopy. The in-situ covalent hybridization of CDs with GO and XNBR latex increases the interactions between the nanofillers and the XNBR latex and also effectively prevents the stacking of GO sheets in XNBR latex. Covalently hybridized XNBR-CDs-GO nanocomposites display higher physico-mechanical properties and thermal stability when compared to XNBR-CDs and XNBR-GO nanocomposites.

ce:section.ce:para: In the recent years, the effects of combination of various types of carbonaceous fillers in polymer materials have gathered increased attention [1,2]. The combinations of different nanofillers in a polymer matrix lead to unique and remarkable new features which may not be possible to achieve using single nanofillers [2–4]. The strategy of using hybrid nanofillers with different geometries have shown to be an effective way to achieve better mechanical, electrical and thermal properties in composite materials [4,5]. It is still an open question to fully understand the synergistic effect produced by the combination of nanofillers [4,5]. The aspects such as ratio of nanofillers in the hybrid filler and geometries of the nanofillers are important factors that govern the reinforcing behaviour of the nanocomposites [6–10]. The hybrid polymer nanocomposites may hold the key for comprehensively rendering high mechanical, electrical and thermal properties which are suitable for different applications [10–16].

Reportedly, methodologies have been developed to create hybrid nanocomposites using various nanofiller mixtures such as graphene oxide (GO)-carbon nanotubes (CNTs) [1,6], graphene nanoplatelets (GNPs)-CNTs [2–4,13], graphite nanoplatelets (GNP)-CNTs [12], reduced graphene oxide flakes (RGOFs)-CNTs [9], CNTs-carbon black (CB) [10], boron nitride nanotubes (BNNTs)-boron nitride nanosheets (BNNSs) [11], GO-nanosilica [5], nanoclay-CNTs [7,8] and GO-carbon nanosphere (CNS) [14] in different polymer matrices such as epoxy resin [1–5,8,10–12] polyvinyl alcohol (PVA) [6,9], polyethylene oxide (PEO) [7], polyetherimide (PEI) [13] and carboxylated acrylonitrile butadiene rubber (XNBR) latex [14].

Im et al. have used wetting process to fabricate GO–CNTs/epoxy composite where the CNTs effectively acts as an interconnector between GO and enhanced the thermal conductivity due to the formation of 3D heat conduction paths [1]. In few cases, the synergetic effects of GNPs and CNTs have been found to significantly increase the tensile properties and thermal conductivity of the epoxy resin [2–4]. This is due to the changes in the contact geometry from 0D point contact to 1D linear contact, which significantly increased the contact surface area within hybrid nanofillers [2–4]. Yue et al. have shown that the hybrid nanofillers from 1D-CNTs and 2D-GNP increases the thermal conductivity of epoxy resin due the synergy originated by bridging of the planar nanoplatelets by the flexible CNTs [4]. Jiang et al. have attributed the toughening effects of 3-aminopropyltriethoxysilane (APTES) functionalized silica nanoparticles attached graphene oxide (ATGO) hybrid nanofillers in epoxy matrix to the high aspect ratio of ATGO in epoxy matrix and also to the presence of greater surface area and many interaction points between the filler and epoxy matrix [5]. Liu et al. have shown that CNTs show greater affinity for nanoclay in epoxy matrix, which in turn facilitates better exfoliation of nanoclay which makes this better suited for a variety of packaging, sensing and shielding applications [8]. Sumfleth et al. have found that the synergistic effects produced by the combination of CNTs and CB on the electrical properties of epoxy resin are mostly based on the formation of network structure in epoxy matrix [10]. Yan et al. have attributed the enhanced thermal–mechanical properties of epoxy/BNNTs–BNNSs composites to the homogeneous dispersion of BNNTs–BNNSs and also to the presence of strong matrix–filler interface interaction [11].

The PVA composite films containing GO dispersed CNTs have been found to show synergistic effects with superior mechanical properties due to the fully exploited reinforcement effect from GO and CNTs via good dispersion in PVA matrix [6]. Kumar et al. have shown that hybridization of GNP with commercially functionalized CNTs in PEI matrix creates extra pathways for heat flow by the formation of a number of junction points among CNTs and between CNTs and GNPs, which significantly increases the thermal conductivity in the hybrid system [13]. Tang et al. have grown and insulated CNTs within nanoclay platelets and created 3D hybrid CNTs/nanoclay nanofillers in PEO matrix that lead to enhanced ion conductivity by better dissociation of lithium salt ions [7]. Shin et al. have studied the synergistic toughening of PVA composite fibers by the self-alignment of RGOFs and CNTs [9]. The toughness of the hybrid fibers prepared using a 1:1 ratio of RGOF to CNTs have been found to exceed that of any material yet reported, including spider silk and kevlar [9]. Tian et al. have shown that GO-encapsulated CNS hybrids significantly increase the dielectric constant (k) value of XNBR latex due to the formation of a segregated filler network and enhancement of interfacial polarization ability of the hybrids after in-situ partial thermal reduction [14].

Carbon dots (CDs) are an emerging class of carbon nanomaterials having size in the range of 1–10 nm [17,18]. The functional groups present on the surface of CDs allow them to get functionalized with various organic, polymeric, inorganic and biological species [19–21]. Various authors have discussed the effect of CDs in polymer matrix for different applications such as optical sensing, biomedical imaging, thermal stability, optical devices and solar devices [22–26]. Recently, in an innovative intervention, we explored carbon dots (CDs) as potential reinforcing nanofiller in XNBR latex where CDs have been found to improve the optical and physico-mechanical properties of XNBR latex [27]. In literature, various authors have shown that incorporation of GO to XNBR latex also improves the mechanical, thermal stability, thermal conductivity, and thermal diffusivity of XNBR latex [28–30].

We hypothesized a synergetic improvement in the physico-mechanical properties of XNBR latex by using CDs and GO as nanofillers in tandem. Therefore, this work attempts to explore the synergetic effect of CDs-GO hybrid nanofiller in XNBR latex. This work is noteworthy for two major reasons. Firstly, to the best of our knowledge, comprehensive studies on the use of hybrid nanofillers in rubber latex have not been previously reported. Secondly, we report a novel strategy to develop an in-situ hybrid nanocomposite by covalently attaching the amine (-NH2) groups of CDs with the carboxyl (-COOH) groups of GO and the –COOH groups of XNBR latex and subsequently to understand the synergistic reinforcement effect of CDs-GO in XNBR latex. The XNBR-CDs-GO hybrid nanocomposites have been found to display superior mechanical, thermal, rheological and notable fluorescence properties when compared to the effect produced by either CDs or GO in XNBR latex.

XNBR latex (XNB-300, acid (-COOH) content∼6%, acrylonitrile (ACN) content∼28% and density∼1 g/cm3) was received from Apcotex Industries Ltd., India. Graphene oxide (GO) dispersion in water with a concentration of 10 mg/ml was purchased from TCI chemicals, Japan. N-hydroxysuccinimide (NHS), 1-(3-dimethylaminopropyl)-3-ethylcarbodiimide hydrochloride (EDC.HCl), citric acid monohydrate and glycine were purchased from Sigma Aldrich, India. All chemicals were used as received and all the solutions were prepared in Milli-Q water.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of in-situ assembled hybrid XNBR-CDs-GO nanocomposites
ce:section.ce:para: The pristine CD nanoparticles were synthesized using citric acid (as a carbon source) and glycine (as a nitrogen source) by hydrothermal heating process [27]. XNBR-CDs-GO hybrid nanocomposites were prepared by covalently conjugating CDs to XNBR and GO through amide coupling mediated by EDC/NHS. The COOH groups present in XNBR and GO were quantified by acid-base titration using a solution of 0.05 M NaOH. The estimation of NH2 groups on the CDs surface was determined by ninhydrin reagent [31]. Firstly, GO was dispersed in XNBR latex using an ultrasonicator for 2 h at a frequency of 25 kHz, followed by continuous stirring using a magnetic stirrer for 6 h (500 rpm) to form uniform XNBR-GO latex suspension. The –COOH groups present in XNBR-GO latex suspension were activated by using 0.5 mmol EDC and 0.75 mmol NHS as the activating agents at a pH of 6 for 1 h. CDs were added to the activated XNBR-GO latex suspension and further stirred using a magnetic stirrer for 4 h (500 rpm) at a pH of 7.4. Unreacted CDs, excess of coupling agents (EDC and NHS) and their by products were removed from hybrid XNBR-CDs-GO composite by dialysis (MWCO- 3.5 KDa). The hybrid XNBR-CDs-GO latex suspension was poured in a flat glass petri dish and dried in air to produce uniform films having thickness in the range of 0.5 ± 0.05 mm. The concentrations of GO were varied from 0.5 to 4 vol % while keeping the concentration of CDs constant (2 parts per hundred ml of rubber latex (phr)) (Table 1 ). Concentration of CDs was kept constant (2 phr) due to the following reason. In our recent communication, it has been shown that incorporation of 2 phr of CDs to XNBR latex prominently improves the optical properties, mechanical properties and thermal stability of XNBR latex [27]. For the sake of comparison, few XNBR-CDs-GO nanocomposites were also prepared by varying the concentration of CDs while keeping the concentration of GO constant (Table 1). To have a clear idea about the synergistic effect of covalent hybridization, XNBR-CDs-GO nanocomposite samples containing 0.5 phr CDs/0.5 vol % GO and 4 phr CDs/4 vol % GO was also prepared (Table 1).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of XNBR-GO and XNBR-CDs nanocomposites
ce:section.ce:para: XNBR-GO nanocomposites and XNBR-CDs nanocomposites were also prepared in order to understand the effects of only GO and only CDs in XNBR latex. This is essential to understand the effects of CDs-GO hybrid nanofiller in XNBR latex when compared to the effect of only GO and only CDs in XNBR latex. XNBR latex containing 0.5, 2, 4 phr of CDs was prepared following the procedure reported in our earlier publication and the samples are shown in Table 1 [27]. The XNBR-GO nanocomposites have been prepared as per the procedure described below. The GO dispersion was mixed with XNBR latex in various quantities (0.5, 2 and 4 vol %) to achieve 0.5 to 4 vol % of GO content in XNBR latex (Table 1). The XNBR-GO mixture was sonicated using an ultrasonicator for 2 h at a frequency of 25 kHz followed by continuous stirring for 6 h using a magnetic stirrer (500 rpm) to obtain uniform XNBR-GO latex suspension. The well dispersed XNBR-GO latex suspension was poured in a flat glass petri dish and dried in air to produce uniform films having thickness in the range of 0.5 ± 0.05 mm.

Powder XRD analysis of pristine CDs were performed in Rigaku TTRX-III diffractometer with Cu-Kα (λ = 1.54 Å) as X-ray source. UV–vis absorption spectra of neat XNBR and XNBR-CDs and XNBR-CDs-GO conjugates in latex stage were obtained on a UV-2550 spectrophotometer (Shimadzu, Japan). Steady-state fluorescence spectra of neat XNBR and XNBR-CDs and XNBR-CDs-GO conjugates in latex stage were measured on a Fluoromax-4 spectrofluorometer (Horiba, Japan). Time-resolved fluorescence emission decays of XNBR-CDs and XNBR-CDs-GO conjugates in latex stage were measured by a time-resolved fluorescence spectrophotometer (LifeSpec-II, Edinburgh Instruments, UK). The fluorescence emission decay measurements were performed at room temperature using picosecond time-correlated single photon counting (TCSPC) technique. Digital images of the films in solid state were captured under UV irradiation using a Canon D600 digital camera. The infrared spectra of the CDs, GO, neat XNBR and XNBR-CDs and XNBR-CDs-GO conjugates in latex stage were measured by attenuated total reflection Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (ATR-FTIR, Perkin Elmer Spectrum 400, USA). X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) measurements of the films in solid state were recorded in an ESCA-3 Mark II spectrometer (VG Scientific Ltd., UK) using Al Kα radiation (1486.6ev).

Mechanical properties of the samples were investigated according to ASTM D 412-C, using a universal testing machine (UTM, Zwick-Roell Z010, Germany) with a crosshead speed of 50 mm/min using a 10 kN load-cell. The data was reported based on five tests for each sample. Rheological flow behaviour of the samples was studied using a modular compact rheometer (MCR 302, Anton Paar, Austria). The dimensions of the samples were in the range of 50 × 10 × 0.5 mm3 (rectangular shape). Samples were tested in torsion mode with a frequency of 1 Hz and strain of 0.1% and the temperature was increased from −50 to 100 °C with a heating rate of 2 °C/min. Thermal stability studies (TGA) were performed in a thermogravimetric analyzer (SDTQ60, TA Instruments, USA) in the temperature range between 30 °C and 800 °C at a heating rate of 20 °C/min, under nitrogen atmosphere. Bulk morphology of the samples was observed using a transmission electron microscope (TEM, JEOL-2100, Japan) with a LaB6 filament at an accelerating voltage of 200 kV. Samples were cryogenically cut using a glass knife (cutting edge of 6–9°) installed in the ultracryomicrotomy (Leica Microsystems Leica Ultra cut UCT, Austria) as per the procedure reported elsewhere [32,33]. Imaging was carried out after casting the 300 nm thick cryosections over the copper grid having 300 mesh size. Surface morphology of the samples was observed by Atomic Force Microscopy (AFM, Agilent 5500 Scanning Probe Microscope, USA) in tapping mode using long tapping mode etched silicon probe (LTESP) tip.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Formation of XNBR-CDs, XNBR-GO and XNBR-CDs-GO nanocomposites
ce:section.ce:para: Citric acid and glycine have been used as the precursors for the synthesis of amine and carboxyl functionalized CDs. XRD analysis of the pristine CDs has been performed to understand the phase structural property (amorphous or graphitic nature) of CDs. The XRD pattern of the CDs is shown in Fig. S1. The XRD pattern clearly shows a peak around 28° which can be attributed to (002) plane of graphite [34]. In addition, a shoulder like peak is observed at around 39.5° which is ascribed to in-plane diffraction of graphene-like structured CDs [34]. The decrease in intensity and increase in full-width half maxima (FWHM) of the presented pattern confirms the predominant amorphous nature of the CDs.

The hybrid XNBR-CDs-GO nanocomposites have been formed by covalently attaching the –COOH groups of the XNBR latex and the GO sheets to the –NH2 groups of CDs using EDC/NHS as water soluble cross coupling agents. Fig. 1 shows the different possibilities of formation of XNBR-CDs-GO hybrid nanocomposites. In the case of XNBR-CDs nanocomposite, the –COOH groups of XNBR latex gets covalently attached to the –NH2 groups of CDs using EDC/NHS as water soluble cross coupling agents [27]. On the other hand, in the case of XNBR-GO nanocomposites, there are strong hydrogen bonding interactions between the oxygen containing functional groups (hydroxyl, carboxyl and carbonyl groups) of GO sheets with the carboxyl groups of XNBR latex [14].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Analysis of covalent conjugation in XNBR-CDs-GO nanocomposite by FTIR and XPS
ce:section.ce:para: FTIR spectra of neat XNBR latex, GO and CDs are shown in Fig. 2 (a). The peak at 3440 cm−1 (3300 cm−1 for GO) correspond to –OH and –NH (only for CDs) stretching vibration while the broad nature of the peak indicates presence of carboxylic groups which has been further confirmed by the presence of –C=O stretching peak at 1710 cm−1 and –OH bending peak at 1410 cm−1. For neat XNBR, peak for –C=O and –C=C- stretching has been co-merged to a peak at 1660 cm−1. Fig. 2 (b) shows FTIR spectra of XNBR-GO, conjugated XNBR-CDs, and XNBR-CDs-GO.

There is a shift in the peak position from 1730 cm−1 to 1734 cm−1 corresponding to the –C=O stretching of carboxylic acid in the XNBR-GO. This indicates the formation of hydrogen bonding between the –COOH groups of XNBR and the –COOH groups and hydroxyl groups (-OH) of the GO sheets. Peak at 1710 cm−1 indicates –C=O stretching of carboxylic acid and peak at 1630 cm−1 is for –C=O stretching of amide and C=C stretching in XNBR-CDs conjugate. For XNBR-CDs and XNBR-CDs-GO, intensity of the peak at 1630 cm−1 increases significantly than that for CDs only along with the appearance of a shoulder at 1565 cm−1, which indicates the formation of amide bond between –COOH groups from XNBR and GO and –NH2 groups from CDs.

Fig. 3 (a–d) shows XPS deconvoluted spectra of XNBR and XNBR-CD-GO. High resolution C1s spectra of XNBR-CD-GO reveals a new peak at 285.8 eV attributed to C–N/C–O. Also in the high resolution N1s spectra of XNBR-CD-GO, peak intensity for amino Nitrogens (399 eV) has been greatly enhanced than the peak intensity for cyano Nitrogens (400 eV). These two information further indicates towards covalent conjugation between XNBR, CDs and GO.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Morphological analysis by transmission electron microscopy (TEM) and atomic force microscopy (AFM)
ce:section.ce:para: TEM image of XNBR+2CDs+2GO sample clearly shows the laminar structure of GO sheet (Fig. 4 (a)). The number of layers in the GO sheet are found to be in the lower range. The width of the individual GO sheet is around 2–3 μm (Fig. 4(a)). The TEM image also clearly depicts the presence of covalent bonded (as discussed earlier) clusters of CDs on the surface of GO sheet (shown by red arrows). In addition, there are CDs which have also interacted with XNBR matrix via. covalent bonding (shown by yellow arrows). The CDs are uniformly found all along the surface of GO sheet having dimension in the range of 20–60 nm (Fig. 4(a)). In general, CDs have tendency to form clusters due to the interaction among themself through the hydrophilic terminal groups on the surface of CDs [27]. In line with TEM image (Fig. 4(a)), the AFM topographic image of the XNBR+2CDs+2GO sample confirms the laminar structure of GO sheet having width in the range of 2–3 μm (Fig. 4(b)). AFM topographic image also shows that the bright CDs nanoparticles are uniformly decorated on the surface of the GO sheet (shown by white circles) having dimension in the range of 20–60 nm (Fig. 4(b)). XNBR+2CDs+2GO sample has been found to display excellent physico-mechanical properties due to the better dispersion and greater interactions between XNBR, CDs and GO (discussed later).

The TEM image of XNBR+2CDs+4GO sample shows laminar structure of GO sheets aggregated and stacked over each other having clusters of CDs on the surface (Fig. 4(c)). The CDs nanoparticles are seen in the range of 20–60 nm on the surface of GO sheets (shown by green arrows). Width of GO sheet is found to be in the range of 2–3 μm and the number of layers in the GO sheet are found to be relatively very high (shown by red dotted circle in (Fig. 4(c)). However, there are no evidence for the presence of CDs in XNBR matrix (Fig. 4(c)). Inset of Fig. 4 (c) shows another TEM micrograph of XNBR+2CDs+4GO sample which clearly shows the presence of CDs in both XNBR matrix and on the surface of GO sheets. This image also confirms the stacking of GO sheets over each other. In line with the TEM image (Fig. 4(c) and inset of Fig. 4(c)), the AFM topographic image of the XNBR+2CDs+4GO sample shows aggregation and stacking of GO sheets with clusters of CDs on the surface of GO sheets (Fig. 4(d)). The width and the dimension of the GO sheets and the CDs are in the range of 2–3 μm and 20–60 nm respectively. The stacking and aggregation of GO sheets can be attributed to the higher concentration of GO in XNBR+2CDs+4GO sample. Therefore, conjugating higher concentration of GO (>2 vol %) to XNBR+2CDs does not lead to good dispersion of GO sheets in XNBR matrix even in the presence of CDs. Accordingly, the sample XNBR+2CDs+4GO display lower phsico-mechanical properties in comparison to the sample XNBR+2CDs+2GO (discussed later).

The exact origin and nature of the CDs fluorescence is still elusive. However, various reports in the literature have attributed the mechanism of fluorescence of CDs to size-dependent quantum confinement effect and surface functionalities and defects [35,36]. Here, since CDs are produced from citric acid and glycine (amine-based nitrogen precursor), its intense blue fluorescence under UV irradiation may be ascribed to polycyclic aromatic hydrocarbon domains and/or molecular fluorophores like derivatives of citrazinic acid and 2-pyridone [37]. The intense blue fluorescence of pristine CDs under UV irradiation is shown in Fig. S2. Optical properties of the samples have been analyzed by UV–visible and fluorescence spectroscopy (Fig. 5 (a) and Fig. 5(b)). From UV–visible spectra, it has been observed that the XNBR+2CDs sample shows a broad absorption while XNBR-CDs-GO hybrid nanocomposites show one shoulder peak at ∼320 nm which can be attributed to absorption due to GO (Fig. 5(a)). The fluorescent CDs induce fluorescence in XNBR latex as shown in fluorescence spectra (Fig. 5(b)).

Reportedly, GO act as a quencher for CDs and other fluorescent moieties due to possible electron transfer process [38]. It has been observed that by increasing the ratio of GO in XNBR-CDs the fluorescence intensity of CDs goes on decreasing (Fig. 5(b)). The fluorescence quenching mechanism of XNBR-CDs by GO was analyzed by measuring the lifetime of the two moieties (Fig. 5(c)). The fluorescence lifetime of XNBR+2CDs was found to be 5.8 ns which get reduced to 5.4 ns in XNBR+2CDs+2GO hybrid nanocomposite. This decrease in lifetime of XNBR+2CDs+2GO hybrid nanocomposite is attributed to nonradiative electron transfer to GO indicating the dynamic quenching of XNBR- CDs [39].

Digital images have been captured under UV irradiation to understand the photo-physical properties of the samples (Fig. 6 ). The change in appearance of neat XNBR latex film from non fluorescent to blue fluorescent was observed on conjugation with CDs. The digital image of XNBR+2GO film shows no change from neat XNBR latex film. However, the digital image of XNBR+2CDs+2GO hybrid nanocomposite film shows less fluorescence in comparison to the XNBR+2CDs film indicating the quenching of fluorescence behaviour of CDs by GO (Fig. 6).

Fig. 7 illustrates the striking synergy developed by covalently conjugating CDs with XNBR latex and GO. From Fig. 7 it is seen that the XNBR+2CDs samples with various ratios of GO show significant increase in tensile stress-strain properties when compared to its unfilled and filled counterparts. The breaking stress value of XNBR latex increases by 280%, 386% and 320% for XNBR+2CDs+0.5GO, XNBR+2CDs+2GO and XNBR+2CDs+4GO hybrid nanocomposites respectively (Table 2). On the other hand, stress at 100% strain value of XNBR latex increases by 720%, 960% and 880% for XNBR+2CDs+0.5GO, XNBR+2CDs+2GO and XNBR+2CDs+4GO hybrid nanocomposites respectively (Table 2). The strain at break (%) values of XNBR latex has been found to decrease with the addition of CDs-GO hybrid nanofiller to XNBR latex (Table 2).

From the above discussion, it is clear that the CDs-GO hybrid nanofiller (containing 2phr of CDs and 2 vol % of GO) has been found to be the optimum concentration for significantly improving the mechanical properties of XNBR latex (Table 2). The breaking stress value and stress at 100% strain value of XNBR+2CDs+2GO hybrid nanocomposite is 52% and 35% higher in comparison to the breaking stress value and stress at 100% strain value of XNBR+2CDs nanocomposite (Table 2). On the other hand, the breaking stress value and stress at 100% strain value of XNBR+2CDs+2GO hybrid nanocomposite is 102% and 77% higher in comparison to the breaking stress value and stress at 100% strain value of XNBR+2GO nanocomposite (Table 2). The superior mechanical properties of the XNBR+2CDs+2GO hybrid nanocomposites can be endorsed to the synergistic effect of CDs-GO in XNBR latex.

The synergistic effect in the hybrid nanocomposites is due to the enhanced interactions between GO and XNBR bound through CDs via. covalent bonding, which leads to good dispersion of GO sheets in the XNBR matrix (see Fig. 4(a) and (b)). The CDs assisted dispersion of GO sheets in XNBR matrix can lead to more efficient transfer of stress due to the large surface area of hybrid nanofillers and also due to the presence of multiple interaction points between the rubber phase and the CDs-GO hybrid nanofillers through the functional groups [11,29]. The strain at break (%) values of XNBR+2CDs+2GO hybrid nanocomposite has been found to be in between the strain at break (%) values of XNBR+2CDs and XNBR+2GO nanocomposites (Table 2). The increase in the strain at break (%) values of the XNBR-CDs-GO hybrid nanocomposites in comparison to the strain at break (%) values of the XNBR-GO nanocomposites has been ascribed to the better dispersion of GO in XNBR latex assisted by CDs. It should be pointed here that when the GO content in XNBR-CDS-GO nanocomposites is increased above 2 vol % (XNBR+2CDs+4GO) the breaking stress value starts to decrease, which is attributed to the aggregation of GO at higher concentration (see Fig. 4(c) and (d)).

For sake of comparison, the mechanical properties of XNBR-CDs-GO conjugates prepared by varying the concentration of CDs (0.5 phr and 4 phr) while keeping the concentration of GO constant (2 vol %) have also been studied. Concentration of GO was kept 2 vol % due to the following reason. Here, it has been shown that incorporation of 2 vol % of GO to XNBR latex prominently improves the mechanical properties of XNBR latex. The breaking stress value (3.6 MPa) and stress at 100% strain value (2.7 MPa) of XNBR-CDs-GO conjugate containing 0.5 phr CDs/2 vol % GO is lower when compared to the breaking stress value (5.8 MPa) and stress at 100% strain value (4.1 MPa) of XNBR-CDs-GO conjugate containing 2 phr CDs/0.5 vol % GO. Similarly, the breaking stress value (5.8 MPa) and stress at 100% strain value (4.3 MPa) of XNBR-CDs-GO conjugate containing 4 phr CDs/2 vol % GO is lower when compared to the breaking stress value (6.3 MPa) and stress at 100% strain value (5.0 MPa) of XNBR-CDs-GO conjugate containing 2 phr CDs/4 vol % GO. Therefore, it is clear that maximum synergetic effect of CDs-GO hybrid nanofiller in XNBR latex can be obtained only when 2 phr of CDs are conjugated with different ratios of GO.

To further confirm the synergistic effect of covalent hybridization the mechanical properties of XNBR+0.5CDs+0.5GO sample have been compared with the mechanical properties of XNBR+0.5CDs and XNBR+0.5GO samples (Table 2). In addition, the mechanical properties of XNBR+4CDs+4GO sample have been compared with the mechanical properties of XNBR+4CDs and XNBR+4GO samples (Table 2). The mechanical properties of XNBR+0.5CDs+0.5GO sample are in between the mechanical properties of XNBR+0.5CDs and XNBR+0.5GO samples (Table 2). On the other hand, the mechanical properties of XNBR+4CDs+4GO sample are markedly higher than the mechanical properties of XNBR+4CDs and XNBR+4GO samples (Table 2). This clearly shows that the synergistic effect can be realized in the hybrid nanocomposites only when a higher concentration of CDs and GO are covalently hybridized to XNBR latex. It is also worth mentioning here that the mechanical properties of XNBR+2CDs+2GO sample are relatively higher than the mechanical properties of XNBR+4CDs+4GO sample (Table 2). This also shows that CDs-GO hybrid nanofiller containing 2phr of CDs and 2 vol % of GO is the optimum concentration for significantly improving the mechanical properties of XNBR latex (Table 2).

Table 3 shows the rheological properties of neat XNBR, XNBR+2CDs, XNBR+2GO and XNBR+2CDs with various ratios of GO. Generally, for most of the polymers, the tan δ peak occurs at the glass transition temperature (Tg) of the polymer. The Tg value of XNBR latex has been found at −15 °C (Fig. 8 (a)). The Tg of XNBR latex moves to a higher temperature (i.e. from −15 °C to −12 °C) with the addition of 2 phr CDs. On the other hand, the incorporation of 2 vol % of GO to XNBR latex does not alter the Tg of XNBR latex (Table 3). The Tg of XNBR latex shifts from −15 °C to −10 °C with the addition of CDs-GO hybrid nanofillers to XNBR latex. The incorporation of CDs-GO hybrid nanofillers prominently shifts the Tg of XNBR latex when compared to the shift in Tg of XNBR latex caused by the addition of either CDs or GO. This can be attributed to the greater interaction of GO sheets with XNBR via. CDs. The chemical bonding between XNBR-CDs-GO increases the connectivity of XNBR matrix with CDs and GO, thus hinders the mobility of the rubber chain leading to the prominent shift in Tg to higher temperatures [30]. In literature, it has also been reported that the tan δ peak cannot be related to the Tg since its position changes based on the value of rubbery modulus, which can change due to the presence of fillers. Therefore, it has been suggested that the Tg of the polymer should be identified from the peak of loss modulus in loss modulus vs. temperature plot [40,41]. Accordingly, here, the Tg values of the neat XNBR latex and XNBR nanocomposites have also been identified from the peak of the loss modulus in the loss modulus vs. temperature plots (Inset of Fig. 8(a)), and the values are reported in Table 3. It is interesting to note here that Tg values obtained from tan δ peak and the Tg values obtained from loss modulus curves shows similar trend.

The reduction in the tan δ peak height value (tan δmax) of XNBR latex by the incorporation of 2 phr of CDs (XNBR+2CDs) is prominent when compared to the reduction in tan δmax value of XNBR latex by the incorporation of 2 vol % of GO (XNBR+2GO) (Table 3). The reduction in the tan δmax value of XNBR latex by the incorporation of CDs-GO hybrid nanofillers is higher in magnitude when compared to the reduction in the tan δmax value of XNBR latex by incorporation of either CDs or GO. The tan δmax value of XNBR latex continuously reduces by increasing the ratio of CDs-GO hybrid nanofillers (Fig. 8(a)). The significant reduction in the tan δmax value of XNBR latex by the incorporation of CDs-GO hybrid nanofillers is because of the presence of very high restriction in the segmental mobility of XNBR rubber chains, which arise due to the excellent interaction between the GO and XNBR bound through CDs by covalent bonding [30,32]. Tg and tan δmax values of the samples are given in Table 3.

Table 3 shows the storage modulus (G') values (at 25 °C) for XNBR, XNBR+2CDs, XNBR+2GO and XNBR+2CDs with various ratios of GO. The G' of XNBR latex increases with the incorporation of 2 phr of CDs to XNBR latex (Fig. 8(b)). G' of XNBR+2CDs nanocomposite is 161% higher when compared to the G' of neat XNBR latex (Table 3). Significant increase in the G' of XNBR latex by the addition of 2 phr of CDs is due to the good dispersion of CDs in XNBR latex via. covalent conjugation. The incorporation of 2 vol % of GO to XNBR latex also increases the G' of XNBR latex (Table 3). However, the G' (10.38 MPa) of XNBR+2GO nanocomposite is lower when compared to the G' (11.8 MPa) of XNBR+2CDs nanocomposite (Table 3).

From Fig. 8 (b) it is seen that there is significant increase in the G' of XNBR latex by the addition of various ratios of CDs-GO hybrid nano fillers to XNBR latex. The G' of XNBR latex increases by 168%, 251% and 184% for XNBR+2CDs+0.5GO, XNBR+2CDs+2GO and XNBR+2CDs+4GO hybrid nanocomposites respectively (Table 3). The G' of XNBR+2CDs+2GO is higher than the G' of XNBR+2CDs+4GO. It should be pointed here that when the GO content in XNBR-CDS-GO nanocomposites is increased above 2 vol %, the G' starts to decrease. At higher concentration of GO in the hybrid nanofiller the GO sheets tend to interact with each other through the polar groups and results in the aggregation of GO-CDs hybrid nanofillers in the XNBR latex which in turn decreases the G' value (Table 3). This is in line with the tensile stress-strain studies (discussed earlier). The G' of XNBR+2CDs+2GO hybrid nanocomposite is 34% higher in comparison to the G' of XNBR+2CDs nanocomposite (Table 3). On the other hand, G' of XNBR+2CDs+2GO is 53% higher when compared to the G' of XNBR+2GO nanocomposite (Table 3). The significantly higher G' of XNBR+2CDs+2GO can be due to the good interaction and dispersion of GO in XNBR matrix mediated through CDs via. covalent bonding (as discussed earlier).

Table 4 shows the thermal properties of neat XNBR latex, XNBR+2CDs, XNBR+2GO and XNBR+2CDs with various ratios of GO. The initial (onset) degradation temperature (Ti) value of XNBR latex increases from 379 °C to 414 °C with the addition of 2phr of CDs (Fig. 9 (a)). On the other hand, with the incorporation of 2 vol % of GO to XNBR latex the Ti of XNBR latex increases from 379 °C to 395 °C (Table 4). The CDs-GO hybrid nanofillers prominently enhance the Ti of XNBR latex when compared to the effect of either CDs or GO on the Ti of XNBR latex. For example, the Ti of XNBR+2CDs+4GO hybrid nanocomposite is approximately 17 °C and 36 °C higher than the Ti of XNBR+2CDs and XNBR+2GO nanocomposites respectively (Table 4). The Ti of XNBR+2CDs+4GO hybrid nanocomposite is approximately 52 °C higher than the Ti of neat XNBR latex (Fig. 9(a) and Table 4). The significant increase in Ti by the incorporation of CDs-GO hybrid nanofillers to XNBR latex is ascribed to the synergistic effect produced by covalently conjugating CDs with XNBR latex and GO. For the sake of clarity, the temperatures at 5 wt% loss (T5) values of all the samples have also been calculated and the corresponding values are reported in Table 4. It is interesting to note that both Ti and T5 values show a similar trend.

The higher residue of XNBR+2CDs sample, when compared to XNBR+2GO sample, is due to the better interaction of CDs with XNBR when compared to the interaction of GO with XNBR. CDs interact with XNBR via covalent conjugation. On the other hand, GO interacts with XNBR via hydrogen bonding. In general, incorporation of nanofiller to polymer tends to increase the amount of residue due to the enhanced interfacial interaction between nanofiller and polymer which hampers the process of degradation and delays the initial degradation [42,43]. It is also well known that the polymer chains close to the nanofiller degrade very slowly and will produce more char [42,43]. In literature, it has also been proposed that the enhanced thermal conductivity of nanotube/polymer composites eases the dissipation of heat within the composite and delays the degradation process and eventually leads to more residues [42,43]. Here, the very high residue values in the case of XNBR-CDs-GO hybrid nanocomposites is due to the in-situ covalent hybridization of CDs with GO and XNBR. In addition, the synergetic effect of CDs-GO slows down the thermal decomposition and enhances the char formation. In literature, the synergetic effect of various hybrid nanofillers such as MWCNT-graphene, MMT-MWCNT and CNTs-functionalized graphene sheets in retarding the thermal decomposition of silicone rubber and enhancing the char formation has been studied [43–45].

The maximum degradation temperature (Tmax) of XNBR latex increases from 458 °C to 460 °C with the addition of 2phr of CDs to XNBR latex (Table 4 and Fig. 9(b)). Similarly, the Tmax of XNBR latex increases from 458 °C to 460 °C with the addition of 2 vol % of GO to XNBR latex (Table 4). XNBR latex containing CDs-GO hybrid nanofillers show relatively higher Tmax when compared to the Tmax of XNBR latex containing either CDs or GO. For example, the Tmax of XNBR+2CDs+4GO hybrid nanocomposite is approximately 5 °C higher than the Tmax of XNBR+2CDs and XNBR+2GO nanocomposites (Table 4). On the other hand, the Tmax of XNBR+2CDs+4GO hybrid is approximately 7 °C higher than the Tmax of neat XNBR latex (Fig. 9(b) and Table 4). The rate of degradation of XNBR latex decreases from 31 to 24 by the addition of 2phr of CDs to XNBR latex (Table 4). On the other hand, rate of degradation of XNBR latex decreases from 31 to 27 by the incorporation of 2 vol % of GO to XNBR latex (Table 4). Incorporation of CDs-GO hybrid nanofillers significantly reduces the rate of degradation of XNBR latex when compared to the effect of either GO or CDs on the rate of degradation of XNBR latex (Fig. 9(b) and Table 4). The significant improvement in the thermal stability of XNBR latex by the incorporation of CDs-GO hybrid nanofillers is due to the covalent bonding between XNBR-CDs-GO, which increases the interactions between the rubber matrix and the hybrid nanofillers. This in turn decreases the thermal motions as well as the degradation probability [30].

Novel in-situ hybrid nanocomposite has been prepared by covalently conjugating the amine groups of CDs with the carboxylic groups of GO and XNBR latex through amide coupling mediate by EDH/NHS. The formation of amide bond has been validated by the presence of characteristic peak corresponding to the amide groups in the FTIR spectrum of XNBR-CDs-GO conjugate and from the C1s and N1s XPS spectra of XNBR-CDs-GO conjugate. TEM and AFM images confirm the presence of covalently conjugated CDs on the surface of GO sheets and also in the XNBR matrix. Morphological studies show uniform dispersion of GO sheets when 2 phr of CDs are covalently conjugated with both XNBR latex and 2 vol % of GO, which in turn prominently enhances the physico-mechanical and thermal properties. On the other hand, GO sheets start to aggregate and stack over each other when 2 phr of CDs are covalently conjugated with both XNBR latex and 4 vol % of GO and subsequently the physico-mechanical properties start to decrease. The XNBR-CDs nanocomposite shows strong fluorescence in the solid film state. On the other hand, XNBR-CDs-GO hybrid nanocomposite display lower fluoresce in solid film state because of the quenching of CDs and other fluorescent moieties by GO due to the electron transfer process. This has been confirmed by measuring the lifetime of XNBR-CDs nanocomposite and XNBR-CDs-GO hybrid nanocomposites. In-situ covalently conjugated XNBR-CDs-GO hybrid nanocomposite show relatively better tensile stress-strain properties (tensile strength and modulus) than that of the pure GO or pure CDs filled XNBR latex. A similar trend has been observed for the rheological (storage modulus) and thermal properties (Ti and Tmax values). This has been attributed to the existence of large surface area and multiple interaction points between the functional groups of XNBR latex and the CDs-GO hybrid nanofiller and also to the efficiency of CDs in preventing the stacking of GO sheets in the XNBR matrix.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: P.R.S. and K.D.K. are indebted to Indian Institute of Technology Patna 801106, India for providing necessary facilities to carry out this work. The company Apcotex Industries Ltd., India is acknowledged for the supply of XNBR latex.

ce:section.ce:para:
The following is the Supplementary data to this article:

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.polymer.2019.05.051.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Tracking the source of carbon dot photoluminescence: aromatic domains versus molecular fluorophores
